ch-09.html.title: Chapter 9 — The Proxy You Didn't Write
ch-09.html.body:
Chapter 9 — The Proxy You Didn't Write
Priya Sharma had been at Glasshouse for exactly one day and four hours when she stopped standup dead.
"Quick one," she said, in the tone of someone who assumed it would be. "I've been reading the triage flow. ReportRepository is an interface. Where's the implementation? I've been looking for an hour, and I'd like to know what I'm doing wrong before I spend a second one."
Silence. Eight engineers around a kanban board, seven years of accumulated folklore, and not one of them could point to a class. Marisol's entire triage queue ran through that repository. Half a year of incidents had been debugged on top of it. Nobody had ever opened its implementation, because there wasn't one to open.
Maya opened her mouth — and caught Elias's eye across the room. A small shake of the head. Afterward, by the kettle, he was explicit. "Don't tell her. Show her how to find out." A pause. "She's yours, by the way. Onboarding buddy. I've already updated the doc."
Eight years of Java and one year of Spring, and my first official act as a mentor is a question I would have flunked in January. At Meridian, the data layer was the one place "where's the implementation?" always had an answer: every query a string of SQL she'd typed, every row-to-object mapping a loop with her name on the commit. Here, apparently, the repository was the magic — and Maya realized she'd been trusting it the way she'd once trusted Boot's startup wiring — auto-configuration conjuring beans off the classpath — politely, and without evidence.
The investigation
They did it the honest way: a breakpoint and a debugger, Priya driving.
Inside TriageWorkflow, where ReportRepository arrived through the constructor, Priya evaluated reportRepository.getClass().
"jdk.proxy3.$Proxy187," she read. "That's not a class. That's an apology."
"It's a JDK dynamic proxy," Maya said. "Spring Data registered a factory bean for your interface at startup. The bean it produces is a proxy that implements ReportRepository — nobody wrote it, it was generated. Step into findById and keep going past the interceptors."
Priya stepped. Three frames of plumbing, and then a real class with real code: SimpleJpaRepository, calling EntityManager.find. "Okay. So that's the implementation. For findById, save, the CRUD set."
"For the methods Spring Data ships. Now the interesting half." Maya scrolled to the methods that existed nowhere at all:
public interface ReportRepository extends JpaRepository {

    // The "latest activity" widget. Compiles. Passed its test.
    Optional findTopByProgramIdOrderByCreatedAt(UUID programId);

    // The reports list. Every page of the infinite scroll pays for this.
    Page findByProgramIdAndStatus(UUID programId,
                                          ReportStatus status,
                                          Pageable pageable);
}

"There's no SimpleJpaRepository method called findTopByProgramIdOrderByCreatedAt," Maya said. "At startup, Spring Data parsed the method name." She dropped a breakpoint in the framework's PartTree constructor and restarted the app. They watched the string go in and a parse tree come out: find, top one, by programId, order by createdAt. Every property was checked against the entity metamodel. The whole thing was compiled into JPQL before the first request ever arrived. "The method name is the query. It's not magic. Let me show you the mechanism."
She heard the sentence as it left her mouth, and stopped. Six months of Elias's green ink, and the catchphrase had changed hands without asking either of them.
Priya, mercifully, was already squinting at the widget code. "Wait. Order by createdAt... which direction?"

⏸ Your call. The build is green and the one-row test has passed for months — before Maya answers: what is findTopByProgramIdOrderByCreatedAt actually returning, why has no test objected, and which -ility got sold when a query became a sentence the compiler doesn't speak?

Which is how, on day four, Priya found the bug everyone senior had missed. The "latest activity" widget on every program dashboard had been showing a report from 2019 for months. OrderByCreatedAt with no Desc is valid in Spring Data's grammar, and the default direction is ascending. Top one of ascending is the oldest. The unit test had a single-row fixture, and one row passes any ORDER BY ever written.
"The method name compiles," Priya said slowly. "It just answers a different question."
That was the part that itched at Maya's hand-wired instincts. The Java compiler only checks that the method name is legal Java; it knows nothing about what the name means. The startup parser does validate the pieces — misspell a property as findByTtile and the context fails fast at boot — but it cannot catch a name that parses cleanly and means the wrong thing. At Meridian a query was a string of SQL the compiler never vouched for, so nobody expected it to: you read the ORDER BY with your own eyes and ran it against real rows before you trusted it. Here the green build had felt like proof, and that reflex was the trap — a derived query is a sentence in a language the Java compiler doesn't speak.
(If you committed at the pause: ascending, so the oldest; one row blesses every sort. The -ility is the deeper cut — Maya names it at the Wall.)
While they were in the logs with show-sql on, Priya asked the second question. "Why does the reports list run two queries per page?"
There it was, under every Page: the SELECT they asked for, and a SELECT count(*) over the whole filtered set that nobody had. The count exists so Page can report totalElements and totalPages, and it runs on every request. On the researcher-facing infinite scroll, nothing ever displayed a total. They were paying for a number they threw away, on every scroll event, against the platform's biggest table.
What the senior sees
At the Wall, under the smoke-cloud proxy drawing with its caption — this. goes around the mirror — Maya gave Priya the stack, top to bottom — "one breath per layer," she promised.
Priya had stopped under the cloud first — she'd read the February postmortem the way new hires read ghost stories. "That's the transaction proxy. The one the bean walked around by calling itself."
"February's ghost in a new coat," Maya said. "That one was CGLIB around a class we wrote, and a this. call never crosses the wrapper — the advice silently never runs. Today's coat has nobody inside it at all. Atrium is full of objects nobody wrote; same lesson, new machinery."
A Spring Data repository is three dispatch rules behind one proxy. CRUD methods route to SimpleJpaRepository. Methods carrying @Query run exactly what you declared — JPQL usually, native SQL when you genuinely need Postgres-specific reach — with named parameters bound by @Param, not by position. Everything else goes through name derivation: parsed once at startup, validated against entity properties, compiled to JPQL. The boot-time validation is the trade you're making — property names are checked, intent is not.
So the team needed a ladder, not a ban. Derived names for the trivial cases. Past that, write the query:
public interface ReportRepository extends JpaRepository {

    Optional findTopByProgramIdOrderByCreatedAtDesc(UUID programId);

    // Infinite scroll: Slice fetches size + 1 and answers hasNext — no COUNT.
    Slice findByProgramIdAndStatus(UUID programId,
                                                  ReportStatus status,
                                                  Pageable pageable);

    // House rule: past two predicates the query is written, not derived.
    // Ordering is pinned here; the Pageable only supplies the window.
    @Query("""
        select new com.glasshouse.atrium.report.ReportSummary(
            r.id, r.title, r.severity, r.status, r.createdAt)
        from Report r
        where r.program.id = :programId
          and r.status = :status
          and r.createdAt >= :since
        order by r.createdAt desc
        """)
    Slice findRecentForTriage(@Param("programId") UUID programId,
                                             @Param("status") ReportStatus status,
                                             @Param("since") Instant since,
                                             Pageable pageable);
}

ReportSummary was a record — five fields, constructed straight from the select clause. "A projection," Maya said. "The query returns exactly these columns, the database does the narrowing, and there's no entity in sight. No entity means no lazy fields waiting to detonate after the session closes, and no over-fetching forty columns to show five. Remember the thousand tiny queries in May? Projections close off that whole family at the boundary." (May, if you skipped it: a loop touched a lazy association on every row — one extra SELECT apiece, a thousand queries for one screen.)
"There are interface projections too," she added, "where Spring generates — yes, another proxy — backed by just the selected columns. Closed ones, where every getter maps to a property, stay efficient. Open ones, with @Value SpEL expressions, quietly fetch the whole entity to evaluate the expression, which un-fixes everything you fixed. We use records. They can't lie."
Priya pointed at the triage search screen spec — severity, status, program, researcher, date range, any combination. "Derived name for that would be a paragraph."
"That's where derivation stops being clever and starts being a hostage note. Dynamic predicates get a custom repository fragment — you write an interface, an Impl class, and Spring Data composes it into the same proxy — built on Specifications or Querydsl, both of which are just composable where-clauses as objects." One breath, as promised.
"And why does any of this need a persistence context underneath?" Priya asked. "Why not just... SQL?"
"Because we chose this engine, and it's worth knowing we chose it. Spring Data JPA rides Hibernate: persistence context, dirty checking, lazy loading — all the power, and all the traps that come with a session. Spring Data JDBC is the same repository grammar with that whole layer deleted — no session, no lazy anything; you load and save whole aggregates, and what you fetched is all you have. For a module with small aggregates and no appetite for surprises, that honesty is a feature. Spring Data Mongo is the grammar again over documents — no joins, so the document has to be the aggregate, modeled for the access pattern, or you suffer. Same interfaces, three engines; the grammar being identical is exactly why you have to know which one you're holding. Atrium's core is relational and shared — it stays JPA."
The architecture under the repositories
Before they left the Wall, Priya pointed at the project tree. "Eleven repositories. Nine tables. Smell, or coincidence?"
"Autopsy," Maya said. "They grew one per table, and per-table repositories are how the graph wins: every query starts anywhere, reaches anything — the soil May's thousand queries grew in. Nobody ever decided what the unit was — and the unit we keep consistent, and change, isn't a table." She took the marker and drew three boxes where there had only ever been entity arrows:


"Report owns its Activity rows. There's an invariant between them — a report's status has to agree with its latest activity — and the lost-update week taught us what guards an invariant: one transaction. So they share a boundary, loaded and committed together; the aggregate is the transaction's natural span. Program and Researcher are other aggregates: a Report carries a programId, never a Program — the @ManyToOne stays mapped, that's just how the foreign key gets written and why findRecentForTriage can say r.program.id, but the rule governs navigation: no code outside a declared read-model query traverses that association across an aggregate line, and domain logic sees only the ID. Inside a boundary, invariants and one transaction; between boundaries, IDs — and, when it matters, eventual consistency."
(The lost-update week, for late joiners: two transactions read the same row, both wrote, and the second silently won — until a @Version check made the race loud.)
"That's DDD," Priya said, in the tone of someone identifying a diet.
"The ten percent that pays rent here: aggregates, and the words. Report, Triage, Disclosure — the vocabulary on this wall is the API of the domain; the day the code says SubmissionRecordDTO and Marisol says report, every meeting pays a translation tax. And Spring Data JDBC? It's this picture enforced — whole aggregates in and out, no lazy graph, because it refuses to believe in one. Who polices the boundary — the framework, or the review — is an architectural choice, not a library preference. We keep the engine and adopt the worldview: repositories per aggregate root, three, not eleven, and reads across a line become projections on purpose. The -ility this week has been circling: evolvability of the data model — the aggregate as the unit of consistency and of change."
Elias drifted past with a teabag and looked at the three boxes considerably longer than the tea required. "Leave those lines up," was all he said. Nobody had a name yet for the map the Wall had just started drawing. (Chapter 14 does.)
Migration day
Friday, 4:55 p.m., the deploy pipeline went red:
Migration checksum mismatch for migration version 41
-> Applied to database : 1486912852
-> Resolved locally    :   89004127
Either revert the changes to the migration, or run repair to update the schema history.

Someone — in fairness, someone moving fast in a team that was suddenly bigger and shipping faster — had "fixed a typo" in V41__add_report_indexes.sql. An index name, cosmetic, already applied to production weeks ago. Flyway records a checksum in flyway_schema_history the moment a versioned migration is applied. From that point the file stops being code and becomes a historical record. Edit history, and validation calls you a liar.
"flyway repair takes thirty seconds," somebody offered, eyeing the clock.

⏸ Your call. 4:55 p.m., pipeline red, flyway repair is thirty seconds away and so is the weekend. Repair now or investigate first — and name the one thing repair can never tell you. Commit.

Idris put the brick on the table. "We can repair the checksum in thirty seconds, or we can know what we're repairing in thirty minutes. One of those happens again at 3 a.m."
Thirty minutes, then: confirm the edit was genuinely cosmetic by diffing the applied SQL against the file, revert the file to match history exactly, and ship the rename as V42 — forward, versioned, boring. The rules went in the runbook before anyone went home: never edit an applied migration; fixes roll forward as new versions; views and functions live in R__ repeatable scripts, which re-run when their checksum changes by design. ("Liquibase, if you're wondering," Maya told Priya, "is the same discipline with a changelog abstraction and rollback choreography — we run Flyway because plain SQL is what we'd be reading at 3 a.m. anyway.")
"Append-only," Idris said, underlining it, "because a versioned migration is a one-way door — walk through, it locks; want something different, take the next door." Then he made it arithmetic. Eight pods, one schema, no maintenance window, every environment replaying the same history in order. Schema evolvability under continuous deploy depends on every database having run the identical sequence of files — which means never rewriting the past. Edit an applied migration and you haven't fixed history — you've forked it: a checksum mismatch if you're lucky, silent staging-production drift if you're not.
And since everyone was already in a room being honest, Maya pulled up the scaling plan and finally did the arithmetic nobody had done in seven years. Each Atrium pod ran Tomcat's 200 worker threads over Hikari's default pool of ten connections. Eight pods × 10 = 80 of Postgres's max_connections = 100. The autoscaling proposal said twelve pods. 120 > 100. The new pods wouldn't make the platform faster; they'd make Postgres start refusing connections — sorry, too many clients already — for everyone, including the healthy pods.
At Meridian there had been no pool to size — DriverManager.getConnection, used, closed, a fresh handshake per query — and nobody ever counted connections, because freight traffic never made anyone. The per-pod story was worse. Under burst, 200 threads contend for ten connections. The losers queue at the pool, time out after Hikari's default thirty seconds, and throw SQLTransientConnectionException — while the database sits at 4% CPU. The pool is sized by the database's limits and the contention in front of it, not by anybody's CPU count. Pool exhaustion is an outage that impersonates a slow database.
It was launch day's equation one layer down — the 503s with idle CPUs, every Tomcat worker parked on one slow vendor. The same math applied here: 200 threads, a query or three behind each Page, funneling into ten connections under a ceiling of a hundred. The queue hadn't gone away; it had simply moved from the connector to Hikari.
The fix
spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 10      # 8 pods x 10 = 80; Postgres max_connections = 100,
                                 # minus headroom for migrations, ops psql, and one emergency
      connection-timeout: 3000   # starve fast and visibly, not at 30s in silence
  jpa:
    open-in-view: false          # still false; projections made it painless

The pull request that closed the week carried more documentation than code. The derived-query rule: explicit @Query past two predicates, and every OrderBy names its direction. Any test of ordering needs at least two rows, out of order — one-row fixtures bless every sort ever written. List endpoints return Slice unless the UI genuinely shows a total; Page costs a COUNT, so it has to be a choice, not a default. Read paths return projections; entities don't cross the service boundary — the last clause of a lesson Maya had been learning since her first green-ink review. Flyway discipline, written down. Hikari, sized by arithmetic instead of superstition, with the scaling plan corrected before it became somebody's 3 a.m. page.
On top sat half a page in docs/adr/ — the week's real decision, made durable:

ADR-009 — Aggregate boundaries.
Context: Repositories had grown one-per-table, queries reached across the object graph from anywhere to anywhere, and pagination and pool sizing both fell over when real data arrived. Decision: Repositories exist per aggregate root — Program, Report (owning Activity), Researcher — never per table; cross-aggregate reads go through projections and read models, never object-graph traversal; references between aggregates are by ID; Flyway migrations are append-only; the Hikari pool is sized from Postgres's max_connections and the launch-day thread math, arithmetic recorded here rather than in folklore. Consequences: The aggregate becomes the transaction's natural boundary — the invariant unit ADR-008 demanded — and a candidate border for whatever Atrium grows into next; the accepted cost: convenient cross-aggregate JOINs stop being free and become deliberate read-model decisions.

The widget fix itself was one word — Desc — and a two-row test. Priya shipped it, four days into the job, with a commit message Maya didn't edit: "Top of ascending is the oldest. Ask me how I know."
⚖ Your move, architect
The lines on the Wall created an immediate problem: the program dashboard reads across all three boxes — reports, researcher names, severity rollups. Yesterday that was one cheerful JOIN through the object graph. Commit before reading on: how do dashboards read across aggregates now?

A — A projection per view. Each dashboard gets its own record read model: one query, exactly its columns, owned by the view.
B — Entity-graph traversal with clever fetching. Keep the object graph for reads; JOIN FETCH and @EntityGraph the exact path each dashboard needs.
C — A separate read database, full CQRS. Writes hit the aggregates; a synchronized read store serves dashboards, shaped purely for reading.

The trade-offs, since you've committed: B re-couples every dashboard to the write model: each new widget needs its own fetch plan against entities whose job is guarding invariants, not rendering — and May already paid that bill: the thousand queries were graph-reads doing a view's job. A is what the team chose: one @Query and one record per view, cheap to add, cheap to delete, the write model free to evolve underneath — the evolvability the aggregates bought, actually spent. C buys the same separation with heavier machinery: synchronization, eventual-consistency UX, a second store to operate and page on — an operability tax. It becomes defensible when reads run orders of magnitude past writes — think 100×, a saturated replica, rollup queries throttling triage; until that signal arrives, C is a door A keeps purchasable. Lena's version went in the notes: "We buy the second database when a customer's dashboard is slow, not when a conference talk was good."
The following Wednesday, Priya gave the Wall tour — the onboarding rite, socket to database and back, every hop named out loud. When she reached the repository she added a hop the diagram hadn't had before: "...and the repository is a proxy nobody wrote, which is fine, because now we know who did." Elias watched Maya watch Priya, and wrote nothing down.
The team was bigger now, and faster. New v2 routes were landing weekly, quicker than any one person was reading them. That would matter soon. Not yet.
The debrief — Ch.9, The Proxy You Didn't Write

Where is the implementation of a Spring Data repository, and how does it execute your queries? Claim: There is no implementation class — Spring Data generates a proxy bean from the interface at runtime. Mechanism: A repository factory registers a JDK dynamic proxy implementing your interface; calls dispatch by rule — CRUD methods to SimpleJpaRepository, @Query methods to your declared JPQL or native SQL, and everything else through method-name derivation, parsed at startup into a query tree, validated against entity property names, and compiled to JPQL. Trade-off: Zero boilerplate and fail-fast validation of property names at boot — but the query lives in a method name, so the compiler checks Java grammar while nobody checks intent, and past two or three predicates the name becomes unreadable. Failure mode: A name that parses but means the wrong thing — OrderByCreatedAt without Desc — compiles, passes a one-row test, and silently returns wrong data for months; the cure is explicit @Query past two predicates and fixtures that can actually fail.

THE ARCHITECT'S LENS

-ilities at stake: Evolvability of the data model — the aggregate as the unit of consistency and change: invariants inside one boundary, references by ID, read models keeping dashboards from freezing the write schema. Append-only migrations are the same -ility at the schema layer; the Hikari arithmetic is availability, bought with division instead of superstition.
The ADR: ADR-009 — aggregate boundaries: repositories per aggregate root (Program, Report+Activity, Researcher); cross-aggregate reads via projections, never graph traversal; references by ID; Flyway append-only; the pool sized from max_connections and the thread math.
Rejected alternatives: per-table repositories with free graph reach (the default that grew — coupling nobody decided); entity-graph dashboards (May's bill, paid twice); full CQRS now (kept purchasable, its 100×-reads signal named in advance); wholesale Spring Data JDBC (boundary enforcement at the price of remapping the platform's core — the worldview was adoptable without the migration).
Fight or lean: Lean on the generated machinery — derivation for trivial queries, SimpleJpaRepository for CRUD, Flyway's checksum as the guardian of history: when it calls you a liar, believe it. Fight the graph — JPA will cheerfully let every entity reach every other forever; the aggregate boundary is discipline the framework never enforces, which is why it lives in review and ADR-009, not in an annotation.

Story anchors

Priya's hour-long hunt for a class that doesn't exist → the repository is a runtime-generated proxy: interface in, JDK proxy out, SimpleJpaRepository behind it.
The 2019 report on the "latest activity" widget → derived-query names compile and parse, but nobody checks intent; missing Desc means top-of-ascending = oldest.
The 4:55 p.m. checksum standoff → applied migrations are history, not code: never edit one; roll forward with a new version, and repair only when you can explain what you're repairing.
Three chalk boxes on the Wall, arrows carrying only IDs → the aggregate is the unit of consistency (invariants inside, one transaction) and of change; cross-aggregate reads are read models, not graph reach — the first lines of what Chapter 14 will call a context map.

Interview ammo

Kill-shot: You write a Spring Data repository interface with no implementation — what actually runs when you call findByStatus(...), end to end? A startup-built JDK dynamic proxy dispatches the call: derivation parsed the method name into a query tree at boot, validated properties against the metamodel, and compiled JPQL that executes through the shared EntityManager — CRUD methods route to SimpleJpaRepository instead.
Follow-up: Why is Page the wrong return type for infinite scroll, and what do you use? Every Page runs a hidden COUNT(*) over the full filtered set to populate totals; Slice fetches size + 1 rows and answers only hasNext, so you skip the count you weren't displaying anyway.
Follow-up: How do you size a HikariCP pool, and what does exhaustion look like? By database arithmetic — pods × pool size + headroom ≤ max_connections, contention measured, never by app CPU count; exhaustion presents as SQLTransientConnectionException after connection-timeout while the DB idles — an outage impersonating a slow database.
Architect follow-up — Repositories: one per table, or one per what? One per aggregate root — the span of one transaction's invariants and the unit the model evolves by; cross-aggregate reads are explicit projections, references cross by ID — which makes today's aggregate tomorrow's candidate context, or service, boundary.

part-05.html.title: Part V — Production
part-05.html.body:
Part V — Production

The nouns, before the story (plain prose — skip freely if you already run Spring)
Security filter chain — the ordered filters that authenticate and authorize every request before MVC sees it. Authentication — who you are. Authorization — what you may do.
JWT — a signed token the client presents on every request; the server validates the signature and claims instead of keeping a session.
CSRF / CORS — browser-world protections: against requests forged on top of ambient cookies, and over which web origins may call your API.
Test slice — booting only one layer (web, JPA, JSON) for a test instead of the whole application. Context cache — Spring reuses a booted test context across tests with identical configuration.
Testcontainers — real dependencies (PostgreSQL) in throwaway Docker containers, so tests verify the database you actually ship.
Actuator / probes — built-in operational endpoints. Liveness — "restart me." Readiness — "route traffic to me."
Metric cardinality — the number of distinct time series a metric produces; each distinct tag value adds one.
Circuit breaker / bulkhead / idempotency — stop calling what keeps failing; cap concurrent calls per dependency; make retries safe by making repeats harmless.

ch-10.html.title: Chapter 10 — The Breach That Wasn't
ch-10.html.body:
Chapter 10 — The Breach That Wasn't
The report arrived on a Tuesday in July, in the Stonethrow queue, between a duplicate XSS and a complaint about markdown rendering. Severity: critical. Author: @sable. Title: "Your guard is posted at a door you no longer use."
Marisol read it twice, then escalated. By ten it was on the war-room screen and nobody was talking.

Any authenticated researcher token can GET and POST to /api/v2/admin/programs/{id}/payout-settings. I changed the bounty multiplier on a program I do not own. I changed it back. Reproduction below. Separately: your API reflects Access-Control-Allow-Origin from whatever Origin I send, with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true. A page I host can read authenticated responses from your API in any logged-in researcher's browser. — s.

Lena set her coffee down carefully. "So our best researcher just told us that on our security platform, a researcher can rewrite the payout math for a bank's program. And then read other people's data from a website she controls."
"It's valid," Maya said. She'd already run the reproduction. Her own JWT — a plain researcher token, no admin role — had sailed straight into the admin endpoint and come back 200 OK with Northbeam's payout configuration.
Idris was three terminals deep in access logs. "Give me twenty minutes. I want to know if anyone else found it first."
Elias was at the Wall, at the Request Journey diagram born in March, the week Sable found the leaking stack traces. He picked up the green pen and drew a thick vertical line in front of the DispatcherServlet box, then a row of small boxes in front of the line — before any controller, before the front door Maya had spent the spring rebuilding. The stretch he was drawing on was the one he'd tapped in March and left deliberately empty — out past the filters, where the same reporter's floor-plan leak had proved that what happens in front of the servlet never reaches a @ControllerAdvice. Same reporter, bigger stakes; the geography lesson had been a forecast.
"Security lives in front of the front door," he said. "I told you in March that chapter ten of your year would happen out here. Welcome to it."
The investigation
Maya pulled up the two suspects. The first was the route guard.

⏸ Your call. A signed, unexpired researcher token came back 200 OK from an admin route — no exception, no error log, nothing visibly broke. Before the code loads, commit: what class of bug lets a request through without anyone deciding to allow it — and did it get there by error, or by drift?

@Bean
SecurityFilterChain api(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    return http
        .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
        .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))
        .authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a
            .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")   // ← still guarding v1
            .anyRequest().authenticated())
        .oauth2ResourceServer(o -> o.jwt(Customizer.withDefaults()))
        .build();
}

She felt the bug before she could name it. At Meridian the check would have been the first line of the handler itself — an if asking who you were before the method did anything; the guard traveled with the code it guarded. Here the rule lived elsewhere. It sat in a filter, matching a URL string. And the URL had moved out from under it.
"March," she said slowly. "When I versioned the public API. I moved the admin controllers under /api/v2/admin/**. The matcher still says /api/admin/**. So a request to /api/v2/admin/programs/{id}/payout-settings matches no specific rule at all. It falls through to anyRequest().authenticated() — and any researcher with a valid token is authenticated."
"Authenticated," Elias said. "Not authorized."
"Right." She wrote it on the Wall under his vertical line. Authn ≠ Authz. "The token's real — signed, unexpired, a genuine Glasshouse researcher. Authentication asks who are you, and the answer was honest. Authorization asks are you allowed — and nobody asked, because the only rule that would have asked was guarding a door we stopped using in March."
(If you called drift at the pause: exactly — nothing malfunctioned; the chain enforced precisely what it said, and what it said had stopped describing the system. Hold that distinction; Elias prices it at the kitchen, later that afternoon.)
The second suspect was older and uglier: a config class with a 2021 commit and a comment that read // unblock the SPA team.
@Bean
CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() {
    var cfg = new CorsConfiguration();
    cfg.setAllowedOriginPatterns(List.of("*"));     // reflects ANY origin
    cfg.setAllowCredentials(true);                  // ...with credentials
    cfg.setAllowedMethods(List.of("*"));
    var source = new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource();
    source.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", cfg);
    return source;
}

"Whoever wrote this," Maya said, "thought CORS was an access-control list."
Idris looked up from the logs. "It isn't?"
"No. CORS is a browser contract. For anything non-trivial the browser sends a pre-flight OPTIONS first — 'may a page from this origin send a POST with an Authorization header?' — and on the real response it reads Access-Control-Allow-Origin to decide whether the JavaScript that asked ever sees the body. The server blocks nothing; it only tells the browser what to permit." She tapped the screen. "Reflecting every origin with credentials tells every browser: let any website read authenticated Glasshouse responses. It's not a lock we left open. It's a sign on the door saying 'all welcome, bring your keys.'"
"No exploitation," Idris announced, leaning back. "Six months of logs. Nobody reached /api/v2/admin/programs/*/payout-settings with a non-admin token except Sable last night — and you, eleven minutes ago. It's a breach that wasn't. She found it first."
The room exhaled. Then the second conversation started — the one Maya would remember longer.
"We fix this quietly," someone from the exec side said. "Patch the matcher, rotate, move on."
Lena was already shaking her head, and so was Elias. "We pay this one in full and we say thank you in public," Lena said. "A security platform that hides its own holes is a punchline waiting for the first researcher who doesn't report what they find. Trust is the product. The glass house only works if we let people see in."
She won. She usually won when she was right about money, and trust was money.
What the senior sees
That afternoon Elias caught Maya by the kitchen, away from the room.
"Your plan. Change /api/admin/** to /api/v2/admin/** and ship."
"It would work," she said.
"You're fixing the hole. Fix the class of hole." He uncapped the green pen. "Next quarter someone adds /api/v3, or a new admin controller nobody updates a matcher for, and the silent fall-through to authenticated() opens again. The model is wrong, not the string."
Her Meridian instinct was no shelter either. Scattered per-handler if checks meant a forgotten if was a forgotten check — fail-open by omission, the same trap in different syntax. So the bug isn't the typo. The bug is that the default is 'allow.'
"Deny by default," she said. "Make the drift structurally impossible instead of carefully avoided."
"Good. And while you're in there — tell me what actually validates a token. Not 'Spring does it.' Show me the mechanism."
She walked it. "The Authorization: Bearer header gets pulled by the BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter — one of the boxes on your line, early in the chain with the other authentication filters. It hands the token to a JwtDecoder — Nimbus under the hood — which verifies the signature against the issuer's JWK set, cached so we're not calling out per request. Then the claim checks: issuer is who we trust, expiry hasn't passed — and audience is us and not some other service sharing the auth server, which isn't automatic: signature, issuer, and expiry come as defaults, but the expected audience we have to declare ourselves — the resource-server audiences property, or our own validator. Only then does a JwtAuthenticationConverter map the claims to authorities — our roles claim becomes ROLE_*, which is the only reason hasRole means anything — and the Authentication lands in the SecurityContextHolder, a ThreadLocal. At the other end, the AuthorizationFilter enforcing the URL rules runs last in the chain, once authentication has had its chance. Authentication first, authorization last. The order is the architecture."
"And if the signature's forged?"
"The decoder throws, the filter's entry point answers 401, and the request dies in the filter chain. The controller never runs. That's the whole point of security living in filters — a bad request never reaches the servlet."
"One more box that isn't on your line," Elias said. "We validate the token. Who mints it?"
"Not us — and that's the half of OAuth2 people skip." The token check she'd once hand-rolled in a servlet filter at Meridian had validated tokens the same codebase minted — issuer and validator were one program, so the split never had a name. "OAuth2 is a protocol with two roles. The authorization server — Keycloak, in our cluster — is the thing that actually authenticates a person or a service and issues the token. We're the resource server: we never see a password, we only validate and enforce. How the token gets issued is a flow — authorization-code with PKCE for humans in browsers, client-credentials for service-to-service — and the old implicit and password grants are deprecated because they leak credentials into places they don't belong. The JWT is just the token's format. OAuth2 is the contract about who mints it and who trusts it — which is why the DSL says oauth2ResourceServer and not parseJwt: it's naming our role in the protocol, not the parsing."
"Good. Now the question Priya's going to ask you at standup tomorrow." Elias almost smiled. "Why is CSRF off?"
The fix
She rebuilt it from the filter chain up and inverted the model.
@Bean
SecurityFilterChain api(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    return http
        .cors(Customizer.withDefaults())                  // explicit allow-list bean below
        .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())                     // ADR-010: no cookie credential
        .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))
        .authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a
            .requestMatchers("/actuator/health").permitAll()
            .requestMatchers("/api/v2/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
            .requestMatchers(HttpMethod.GET, "/api/*/reports/**").authenticated()
            .anyRequest().denyAll())                      // deny-by-default: silence means 403
        .oauth2ResourceServer(o -> o.jwt(j ->
            j.jwtAuthenticationConverter(jwtAuthenticationConverter())))
        .build();
}

anyRequest().denyAll() did the real work. A new route was unreachable until someone wrote an explicit rule for it, so a forgotten path failed closed — 403, not 200. The matcher couldn't drift open; there was no open to drift to.
The kitchen walkthrough had named the one claim check the framework wouldn't assume. The diff that delivered it was two lines of YAML.
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: https://auth.glasshouse.io/realms/glasshouse
          audiences: atrium   # ADR-010: the one claim validation the framework
                              # will NOT assume — a valid token minted for another
                              # service must bounce off this line

And hasRole("ADMIN") was only a real rule because the chain no longer ended in Customizer.withDefaults(). Out of the box the converter maps the scope/scp claim to SCOPE_* authorities and nothing else. The default speaks SCOPE_, not ROLE_. Keycloak's realm roles live in their own claim. The mapping was hers to declare, like the audience.
@Bean
JwtAuthenticationConverter jwtAuthenticationConverter() {
    var roles = new JwtGrantedAuthoritiesConverter();
    roles.setAuthoritiesClaimName("roles");   // Keycloak realm roles, flattened into the token
    roles.setAuthorityPrefix("ROLE_");        // the default maps scope -> SCOPE_*, never ROLE_*
    var conv = new JwtAuthenticationConverter();
    conv.setJwtGrantedAuthoritiesConverter(roles);
    return conv;
}

But URL rules were coarse. "Any researcher can GET reports" wasn't the rule. The rule was "a researcher reads their own reports; a triager transitions status; an admin manages programs." That belonged on the domain methods, as defense in depth. Records at the boundary, not entities.
@Service
public class ReportService {

    @PreAuthorize("hasRole('RESEARCHER') and @reportAccess.owns(#reportId, authentication)")
    public ReportView read(long reportId) { /* load, map to record */ }

    @PreAuthorize("hasRole('TRIAGER')")
    public void transition(long reportId, Status next) { /* ... */ }

    @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
    public void updatePayoutSettings(long programId, PayoutSettingsUpdate update) { /* ... */ }
}

@reportAccess was a small bean SpEL could call by name. The ownership logic lived in one tested place instead of copy-pasted strings.
She paused at @PreAuthorize and felt February all over again — the smoke-cloud drawing. "Method security is an AOP proxy," she told Priya, who'd wandered over. "Same proxy as @Transactional. If ReportService calls its own updatePayoutSettings through this., the proxy never sees the call and the check evaporates." The February discipline already had it covered — ADR-003 had made this.-invoked annotated methods a review finding, so @PreAuthorize simply joined the list the smoke cloud predicted. She made a note for the tests anyway: assert the rule fires on the injected bean, never on a hand-constructed one.
The CORS fix was the smallest diff and the biggest mindset change: ACL out, named origins in.
@Bean
CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() {
    var cfg = new CorsConfiguration();
    cfg.setAllowedOrigins(List.of("https://app.glasshouse.io"));   // named, not reflected
    cfg.setAllowedMethods(List.of("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"));
    cfg.setAllowedHeaders(List.of("Authorization", "Content-Type"));
    cfg.setAllowCredentials(true);                                  // safe now: origin is fixed
    var source = new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource();
    source.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", cfg);
    return source;
}

Then CSRF — Priya's standup question, exactly as Elias predicted.
"Because JWT," Priya said.
Elias waited. He was good at waiting. Maya let the silence work, then nudged. "Go a layer down. Why does a bearer token make CSRF moot?"
Priya thought. "CSRF works because... the browser sends the cookie automatically? On any request to our domain, even from an attacker's page?"
"That's it," Maya said. "CSRF exploits ambient credentials — a session cookie the browser attaches to every request to our origin whether the user meant to send it or not. The attacker's page can't read the response, but it can cause a state-changing request, and the cookie rides along for free — hence the CSRF token, a value that page can't obtain. But our credential lives in the Authorization header — JavaScript has to attach it deliberately; the browser never sends it ambiently. No ambient credential, no CSRF surface — leaving it on would guard nothing and break our own legitimate POSTs." She wrote the rule on the Wall: cookie session → CSRF on; pure token API → CSRF off. "Same logic kills session fixation: in the cookie world an attacker pre-plants a session ID and waits for the victim to log into it — why Spring Security rotates the ID at login. No session, nothing to fixate. The skill isn't 'JWT is safer.' The skill is knowing which credential is ambient and defending that." The ADR recorded the rest: off because no cookie credential; revisit the day a cookie appears.
Before the Wall session broke up, Elias handed her the green pen. "One more drawing. Not the fix — the architecture. Walk the request again, but this time don't tell me what each layer does. Tell me what each layer is allowed to trust from the one before it."
She started at the far left, where the Wall met the window.


"Zero standing trust," she said, stepping back. "Identity is cryptographic or absent, and silence past the matchers is a 403. Each layer re-checks instead of trusting the one upstream: the chain settles who; the method layer re-asks whether — role and ownership — even though the request is already in. The chain authenticates, the method authorizes, the data layer assumes nothing."
"And the arrows. What crosses them?"
"Claims. Every arrow is a token boundary, and audience is the contract between zones — the claim naming which boundary the token was minted to cross. A token Keycloak issues for some other service carries a perfectly valid signature; it bounces off Atrium only because the audience check is ours to declare. Which is why it isn't a default."
"Now say the quiet part. What failed in July — the matcher, or the model?"
"The model. Fail-open is never designed; it accumulates — every refactor that outruns a URL string widens it, and nobody approves the purchase because nobody sees a diff. Deny-by-default makes fail-closed a structural property instead of a maintained one. That's the -ility on this wall — security as architecture, not as vigilance: making the safe thing the lazy thing." She wrote that last sentence under the zones, and then, before it cooled, the half-page:

ADR-010 — Security architecture.
Context: @sable filed a valid critical — an admin route reachable without a role check, plus a credentialed wildcard CORS policy; the URL rules had drifted from the routes they guarded. Decision: Atrium is a stateless OAuth2 resource server (Keycloak issues, Atrium validates — signature, issuer, declared audience, expiry); the chain ends in anyRequest().denyAll(); method security adds role-plus-ownership rules as defense in depth; CSRF stays off for the token API on the record — no ambient cookie credential — and CORS is a named allow-list; access tokens run short with refresh rotation. Consequences: A forgotten route now fails closed (403) instead of open, at the price of an explicit rule for every new route — and a leaked token stays valid until expiry, a trade-off accepted in writing rather than discovered in an incident.

Afterward, in the corridor, Elias said quietly: "That. That's the job. Consider this your notice — months before anyone else gets theirs." And he told her — privately, long before any all-hands would — that he was stepping back at year's end, and that this rebuild had been hers for a reason.
The password path was boring in the best way: DelegatingPasswordEncoder, BCrypt with the {bcrypt} prefix at strength 12. "If this had been plaintext, or anything reversible," she told Priya, "the whole report would be a footnote — the storage itself would be the breach. BCrypt is deliberately slow, and the strength is a log scale: 12 means 2¹² rounds, so each increment doubles the cost. Milliseconds per login for us; an economic dead end for whoever steals the dump. And the prefix means we can migrate to Argon2 later without invalidating a single existing hash."
The validation checklist went up on the Wall next to the filter boxes — signature → issuer → audience → expiry → SecurityContext — with the uncomfortable last line: a stolen token stays valid until it expires. Statelessness cuts both ways.
⚖ Your move, architect
That last line is a fork, and ADR-010 had to take a side. Sable's next report, in some version of the future, is a leaked researcher token. Before reading what they chose, commit to a revocation posture:

A — Short TTL + refresh rotation, nothing else. Access tokens live minutes; refresh tokens rotate on every use, and a replayed refresh token kills the whole family. The exposure window is priced and accepted.
B — A denylist on the hot path. Revoked token IDs (jti) in a shared store, checked on every request. Real revocation, today.
C — Opaque tokens + introspection. The resource server asks the authorization server about every token it sees; Keycloak becomes the per-request source of truth.

The trade-offs, since you've committed: B gets real revocation but gives up statelessness — the shared store joins every request's critical path, adding latency and a new failure mode: when the denylist store is down, do you fail open or closed? You've just rebuilt the question this chapter answered. C gives up availability: the auth server joins every request's critical path — April's Cortexa lesson, where one synchronous dependency's bad afternoon spent the whole thread pool, the coupling ADR-006 taught the team to price. A is what they chose and what ADR-010 records: a bounded exposure window — fifteen minutes, knowable, in writing — in exchange for the horizontal scaling that justified JWTs in the first place. Every option sells an -ility; the architect's answer is naming which. The mature answer is never "you can't revoke a JWT." It's "we decided not to, here is the price, and here is the receipt."
They shortened access tokens to fifteen minutes with refresh rotation, and recorded the heavier options — a denylist of revoked token IDs, live introspection — as the day-two path, each buying back control by selling statelessness.
And one more mandate, hers: a security test per route — 401 without a token, 403 with the wrong role, 200 with the right one. The thing that would have caught the drift the day it shipped. Idris read the plan over her shoulder and said, mildly, "That's a few hundred new test classes on a CI already creeping past twenty minutes." Nobody heard him. They would, in August.
Sable's retest came back two days later, one line: "Clean. And thank you for publishing. — s."
The postmortem went out publicly with Maya's name on it — the matcher, the CORS bean, the rebuild, the bounty paid in full. She left July carrying two things: a security model anyone could explain from the Wall, and a date in December nobody else knew yet.
The debrief — Ch.10, The Breach That Wasn't

JWT stateless auth — where in the filter chain is the token validated, and why is CSRF protection usually disabled?
Claim: In a stateless JWT API the token is validated in the security filter chain — before the DispatcherServlet ever runs — and CSRF protection is disabled because there is no ambient cookie credential to forge. Mechanism: The BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter extracts the Authorization: Bearer header and hands it to a JwtDecoder, which verifies the signature against the issuer's cached JWK set, then checks issuer, expiry, and (opt-in) audience; on success a converter maps claims to authorities and populates the SecurityContextHolder, and the AuthorizationFilter at the end of the chain enforces the access rules. Browsers auto-attach session cookies cross-site — that is CSRF; a header token is never sent ambiently, so a CSRF token would guard nothing. Trade-off: Stateless tokens scale horizontally with no session store, but you surrender server-side revocation — the token is good until expiry no matter what you learn afterward. Failure mode: A leaked token stays valid for its full TTL — the JWT revocation problem. Mitigate with short TTLs plus refresh rotation, a per-request denylist of token IDs, or live introspection — each trading statelessness back for control.

THE ARCHITECT'S LENS

-ilities at stake: Security as architecture, not vigilance: trust zones with explicit token boundaries, and fail-closed as a structural property (denyAll()) versus fail-open as accumulated drift — the hole was opened by evolvability (a March refactor outrunning a URL string), which is why the fix is a model, not a matcher. Scalability is what statelessness buys; the revocation window is what it costs, priced in writing. Idris's unheard remark is operability debt accruing, due in August.
The ADR: ADR-010 — security architecture: stateless OAuth2 resource server (Keycloak issues, Atrium validates — signature, issuer, declared audience, expiry), anyRequest().denyAll() at the chain, method security with ownership rules as defense in depth, CSRF off on the record, CORS as a named allow-list, fifteen-minute tokens with refresh rotation.
Rejected alternatives: patching the matcher string and shipping (fixes the hole, not the class of hole — the drift re-opens at /api/v3); per-route annotations as the only layer (fail-open by omission — the same drift in annotation syntax); a denylist or introspection today (each buys revocation back by selling statelessness or availability — recorded as the purchasable day-two path instead).
Fight or lean: Lean on the resource-server machinery — BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter, the Nimbus decoder, the cached JWK set; hand-rolled token validation is how signature-bypass CVEs are born. Fight the permissive defaults you wrote yourselves — the wildcard CORS bean and the authenticated() fall-through were Glasshouse's ink, not Spring's — and remember the two checks the framework will not assume for you: the audience, and denyAll() itself.

Story anchors

The matcher guarding /api/admin/** while the routes moved to /api/v2/admin/** → URL-string rules drift; anyRequest().denyAll() makes a forgotten route fail closed instead of open.
The CORS bean reflecting any origin with credentials, "to unblock the SPA" → CORS is a browser contract, not a server ACL; name your trusted origins.
Sable's researcher token coming back 200 OK from the admin endpoint → authentication (who you are) is not authorization (what you may do).
The trust-zone map on the Wall — "the chain authenticates, the method authorizes, the data layer assumes nothing" → zero-trust posture: each layer re-checks instead of trusting upstream, and the audience claim is the contract that says which boundary a token was minted to cross.

Interview ammo

JWT stateless auth — where in the filter chain is the token validated, and why is CSRF usually disabled? → In the BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter, before the servlet — signature, issuer, expiry, opt-in audience, then SecurityContext; CSRF is off because a header token isn't ambient the way a cookie is — it goes back on the day a cookie appears.
Your anyRequest().authenticated() let a researcher reach an admin route — what's the real fix? → Authenticated isn't authorized: invert to denyAll() with explicit allows so unknown routes fail closed, then layer @PreAuthorize ownership rules as defense in depth — remembering method security rides the same self-invocation-prone AOP proxy as @Transactional.
How do you revoke a JWT before it expires? → You don't, by design — you pick a poison: short TTL plus refresh rotation (cheap, small exposure window), a denylist of token IDs (a per-request lookup), or introspection (full control, fully stateful again).
Architect follow-up — Your tokens can't be revoked; defend that to a security review. → Name the sale and show the receipt: ADR-010 prices a bounded fifteen-minute exposure window (refresh rotation with reuse detection killing the family) against the statelessness that lets the API scale with no shared store on the hot path — and records denylist and introspection as purchasable day-two options, so "can't revoke" becomes "decided not to, in writing."
